concept.title: Retrieval, Reranking, and Answer Generation
concept.shortdesc: How the chatbot searches the vector store with metadata filters, reranks results with a cross-encoder, and generates cited answers grounded entirely in the training documentation.

section.title: How does metadata-filtered retrieval work?
section.p: When a user asks a question, the chatbot performs a two-phase retrieval:

ol.li: Intent classification: The query is classified as a question type (explain, how-to, troubleshoot, reference, definition)
Filtered vector search: The query embedding is compared against the vector store, but only chunks matching the metadata filters are considered

section.codeblock:
User query: "How do I set up a CI/CD pipeline for DITA builds?"
Intent detected: how-to Metadata filters applied: content-intent IN (how-to, tutorial) content-domain IN (devops, publishing)
Vector search: top_k=8, score_threshold=0.72
Results before filtering: 3,200 chunks Results after filtering: 140 chunks Results after vector search: 8 chunks
section.p: Without metadata filters, the search would scan all 3,200 chunks and might return concept explanations instead of task instructions. The metadata filter narrows the search space by 96%, dramatically improving both speed and relevance.

section.title: How does chatbot-priority boosting affect results?
section.p: The chatbot-priority metadata field applies a score multiplier to retrieval results:

row.entry: Core content surfaces first; user-facing tasks and key concepts get top placement
Standard ranking; supporting content appears in natural order
Peripheral content (historical notes, edge cases) is deprioritized

section.p: This means a chunk with a vector similarity score of 0.85 and high priority gets an effective score of 1.105, while a chunk at 0.88 with low priority gets 0.704. The high-priority chunk ranks first despite a lower raw similarity score.

section.title: Why is cross-encoder reranking necessary?
section.p: Vector similarity search is fast but imprecise — it finds chunks that are topically similar but may not actually answer the question. A cross-encoder reranker is a second model that reads the query and each candidate chunk together and scores how well the chunk answers the query.

ol.li: Cross-encoder scores each (query, chunk) pair on a 0-1 relevance scale
Chunks are re-sorted by cross-encoder score
Only chunks above the reranker threshold are passed to the LLM

section.p: In testing, cross-encoder reranking improved answer accuracy by 18–24% compared to vector search alone, because it catches cases where surface-level word overlap misleads the embedding model.

section.title: How does the LLM generate grounded answers?
section.p: The answer generation prompt is constructed from the top-ranked chunks:
section.codeblock:
SYSTEM PROMPT: You are a DITA training assistant. Answer the user's question using ONLY the provided context chunks. If the context does not contain the answer, say so. Cite your sources using [topic_id § section_id] format.
CONTEXT CHUNKS: [1] topic_id: github-actions-cicd section: pipeline-setup type: task "To set up a CI/CD pipeline for DITA builds..."
[2] topic_id: docker-basics section: container-builds type: task "Docker containers enable reproducible DITA builds..."
[3] topic_id: dita-publishing section: automated-builds type: concept "Automated publishing pipelines ensure..."
USER QUESTION: "How do I set up a CI/CD pipeline for DITA builds?"
section.p: The generated answer cites specific sections, allowing users to verify the information:
section.codeblock:
GENERATED ANSWER: To set up a CI/CD pipeline for DITA builds, follow these steps:
1. Create a GitHub Actions workflow that triggers on pushes to your main branch [github-actions-cicd § pipeline-setup]
2. Use a Docker container with DITA-OT pre-installed to ensure reproducible builds [docker-basics § container-builds]
3. Configure the pipeline to run `dita -i your.ditamap -f html5 -o output/` as the build step [github-actions-cicd § pipeline-setup]
See also: Automated Publishing (dita-publishing § automated-builds)

section.title: How does the glossary enhance answers?
section.p: When a query or answer contains terms defined in the DITA glossary, the chatbot can:

ul.li: Inline definitions: Automatically expand abbreviations the first time they appear ("DITA-OT (DITA Open Toolkit)")
Link to glossary entries: Add a "Key terms" section at the bottom of the answer
Resolve synonyms: Map user terminology to the canonical terms in the controlled vocabulary, improving retrieval matching
Scope warnings: Flag when a term applies to a specific version ("Note: keyref requires DITA 1.2+")

section.p: This is only possible because DITA glossary entries are structured data with glossterm, glossdef, glossSurfaceForm, and glossAlt — not just a flat list of term-definition pairs.
